topic: How Energy Efficiency Affects Food Chains
title: Why only part of energy reaches the next level

imageCredit.creator: chris 論 (through works of J. Patrick Fischer, C. Schuhmacher, Madprime, Luis Fernández García, Luis Miguel Bugallo Sánchez, chung-tung yeh, Susanne Heyer and Simon Andrews)

lesson.learn:
Energy transfer between trophic levels is inefficient because living things use most of the energy they take in just to stay alive. They breathe, move, grow, repair tissue, and maintain body temperature. Only a fraction becomes new biomass that can be passed on to the next level.

That is why food chains get shorter as you move upward. A huge amount of energy enters the system through producers, but much less is available for top consumers. The result is a pyramid rather than a straight line of equal power.
lesson.simpler: Animals use most of the energy they eat just to live. That leaves only a small part for the next level in the food chain.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Food chains are limited because energy is lost at every step.
lesson.deeperDive:
The familiar "10 percent rule" is a rough way of saying that each transfer is highly inefficient. The exact number changes with the ecosystem, but the basic truth stays the same: energy disappears as heat and metabolism at each step. Producers capture sunlight, herbivores keep a portion, and predators get only what is left.

This is why ecosystems support far more plants than top predators. Energy efficiency shapes everything from population size to the length of food chains and the design of ecological pyramids.
lesson.funFact: Most of an animal's energy never reaches the next level.
references: Ecology textbooks - energy transfer in ecosystems

people: Farm scientists - think about efficient food production
places: Forests - energy moves through many layers
Oceans - energy flow supports marine food webs
concepts: Trophic level - a position in the food chain
Biomass - living material that stores energy
Metabolism - energy used for life processes
Energy pyramid - less energy higher up the chain
